intro: Let's start by understanding what happened in your 2025 tax year. This helps us focus only on the sections that matter for your situation.

screens.title: Which of the following happened in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Don't worry if you are unsure — we'll ask follow-up questions for each. We'll cover standard items like RRSP, TFSA, and common credits regardless of your selections.

options.label: I moved to a new city or province
I changed jobs or started a new job
I got married, separated, or divorced
I had a child or adopted
I started a business or became self-employed
I or a family member has a disability
I attended school or a post-secondary program

branch_completion_summary.message: Here's what we'll cover based on your 2025 situation.

intro: First, a few basics about your situation. These help us personalize everything that follows.

screens.title: Where did you live on December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: Your province of residence on December 31 determines your provincial tax rates and which provincial credits apply for the full year — even if you moved mid-year.

options.label: I became a Canadian resident in 2025

ON.message: Ontario residents qualify for the Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) under OTA s.103.1, the Low-income Individuals and Families Tax (LIFT) Credit under OTA s.103.4, and the Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant under OTA s.104.1. We will cover all three at the end of your interview.

BC.message: British Columbia provincial credits will be noted where applicable. All federal T1 items covered in this interview apply regardless of province.

AB.message: Alberta provincial credits will be noted where applicable. All federal T1 items covered in this interview apply regardless of province.

QC.message: Quebec residents file a separate provincial return with Revenu Québec in addition to the federal T1. This interview covers federal items only. Quebec-specific items should be reviewed with a Quebec-licensed preparer.

other_province.message: Provincial credits for your territory or province will be noted where applicable. All federal T1 items covered in this interview apply regardless of province.

newcomer.message: Welcome to Canada. As a new resident, your Canadian tax obligation begins on your date of arrival. Your return covers only that partial year. We will factor your arrival date into credit eligibility and pro-ration calculations throughout the interview.

screens.title: What was your marital status on December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: CRA determines most credits and benefit entitlements based on your status on December 31. If your status changed during 2025, select your status at year-end — we will ask about the change separately if relevant.

married.message: Your spouse's net income affects the spousal amount credit under ITA s.118(1)(a), the GST/HST Credit, Canada Child Benefit, and several other income-tested credits. We will ask for their income range when relevant.

common_law.message: Common-law partners are treated identically to married spouses under the ITA after 12 continuous months of cohabitation, or immediately if you have a child together. Your partner's net income affects the same credits as a married spouse.

separated.message: If you separated in 2025, your December 31 status determines credit eligibility. Support payment deductibility under ITA s.60(b), the eligible dependant amount under ITA s.118(1)(b), and several credits are affected. We will cover these if the Marital Status Change branch applies.

divorced.message: Divorce affects support payment deductibility under ITA s.60(b), credit eligibility, and property transfer rules under ITA s.73(1). We will cover the relevant items.

widowed.message: The death of a spouse triggers specific rules — RRSP spousal rollover under ITA s.146(16), deemed disposition of capital property under ITA s.70(5), and survivor benefit reporting. We will flag the key items throughout.

screens.title: What is your age as of December 31, 2025?
screens.instruction: Age determines registered plan obligations, credit eligibility, and pension income splitting availability.

under_18.message: Under 18 noted. If you have employment, self-employment, or other income you are still required to file a T1 return. Filing also establishes RRSP contribution room for future years.

65_to_71.message: At age 65 or older you may qualify for the Age Amount credit under ITA s.118(2) — worth up to $8,790 federally in 2025, subject to a net income phase-out starting at $42,335. You also become eligible for the Pension Income Credit under ITA s.118(3) and pension income splitting under ITA s.60.03. We will cover all of these.

over_71.message: At age 72 or older your RRSP must have been converted to a RRIF or annuity by December 31 of the year you turned 71 under ITA s.146(2)(b.4). If conversion did not occur the full RRSP balance is included in income. You also qualify for all age 65+ credits. We will address RRSP conversion in the Retirement and Age Milestones sections.

screens.title: Do you support any dependents?
screens.instruction: Dependents include children under 18, full-time students, elderly parents, or other family members you financially support. This may unlock additional interview sections even if you did not flag a life event.

true.message: Dependents noted. Potential items include child care expenses under ITA s.63(1), the eligible dependant amount under ITA s.118(1)(b), the caregiver amount under ITA s.118(1)(c.1), and the Canada Child Benefit. We will ask for details in the relevant sections.

intro: Let's cover your employment income for 2025. This includes any job where you received a T4 slip.

screens.title: How many employers did you work for in 2025?
screens.instruction: Include part-time, casual, and seasonal employment. Each employer issues a separate T4 slip. Employment income is taxable under ITA s.5(1).

options.label: I had no employment income in 2025

none.message: No T4 employment income noted. We will cover other income sources in the next section.

one.message: One T4 to report. Employment income reported on Line 10100.

two.message: Two T4s to report. If you changed jobs mid-year and both employers deducted CPP and EI premiums independently, you may have overpaid. Your preparer will reconcile — any overpayment of CPP premiums generates a refund under ITA s.118.7.

checklist_update.note: Two T4s — preparer to confirm CPP/EI over-deduction

three_plus.message: Multiple T4s noted. CPP and EI over-deduction is common with three or more employers. Excess CPP premiums are refundable under ITA s.118.7. Excess EI premiums are refunded automatically by CRA.

checklist_update.note: Multiple T4s — confirm CPP/EI over-deduction with preparer

screens.title: Did you work in Canada for the full year in 2025?
screens.instruction: Partial-year employment affects RRSP contribution room, LIFT Credit eligibility, and certain credit calculations that are based on earned income.

options.label: Yes, I worked in Canada for the full year
No, I arrived or started working part-way through 2025

true.message: Full-year Canadian employment confirmed.

false.message: Partial-year employment noted. RRSP contribution room is based on prior year Canadian earned income. LIFT Credit eligibility is based on Ontario employment income only. We will flag all affected items.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm arrival date and Canadian employment start date — required for RRSP room calculation and pro-rated credit eligibility

screens.title: Approximately what was your total employment income in 2025?
screens.instruction: An estimate is fine. This helps determine which income-tested credits and deductions apply. This information is not stored or transmitted.

options.label: I would prefer not to say

under_20k.message: At this income level you may qualify for the Canada Workers Benefit under ITA s.122.7, the GST/HST Credit under ITA s.122.5, and the Ontario LIFT Credit under OTA s.103.4. We will confirm eligibility for each.

20k_to_30k.message: At this income level the Ontario LIFT Credit likely applies under OTA s.103.4 and the Canada Workers Benefit may apply depending on family net income under ITA s.122.7.

30k_to_50k.message: The LIFT Credit begins to phase out around $38,500 in net income under OTA s.103.4. We will flag it as possible for your preparer to confirm based on your actual net income.

50k_to_100k.message: Good. This helps calibrate your RRSP deduction benefit and applicable credits.

over_100k.message: At this income level several phase-out thresholds apply — Age Amount reduction under ITA s.118(2) if 65+, OAS repayment under ITA s.180.2 if applicable, and the basic personal amount enhancement. We will flag each relevant item.

prefer_not_to_say.message: Understood. We will flag all income-sensitive credits as possible — your preparer will confirm which apply based on your actual net income.

screens.title: Did you receive employment income from a foreign employer in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes income paid in foreign currency or from a company based outside Canada. Foreign employment income is fully taxable in Canada under ITA s.5(1) even if tax was withheld abroad.

true.message: Foreign employment income is taxable in Canada under ITA s.5(1) and must be converted to Canadian dollars using the Bank of Canada annual average exchange rate. Tax paid to a foreign government may generate a Foreign Tax Credit under ITA s.126(1) to avoid double taxation.

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain foreign employment income statement, confirm currency conversion rate used, and gather proof of foreign tax withheld

screens.title: Did you work from home in 2025?
screens.instruction: Employment home office expenses are deductible under ITA s.8(13) only if your employer required you to work from home and certifies this on Form T2200 under ITA s.8(10). Choosing to work from home does not qualify.

options.label: Yes — my employer required me to work from home

yes_required.message: You may deduct a proportionate share of rent, utilities, internet, and maintenance under ITA s.8(13). Your employer must complete Form T2200 under ITA s.8(10) certifying the requirement. The workspace must be where you principally perform your employment duties, or used exclusively for employment and regularly for meeting clients.

yes_choice.message: Home office expenses are only deductible if your employer required you to work from home and certifies this on Form T2200. Working from home by personal choice does not qualify under ITA s.8(13).

screens.title: Has your employer provided or agreed to sign Form T2200?
screens.instruction: Form T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment — is a mandatory supporting document under ITA s.8(10). Without it CRA will deny employment expense claims on audit.

options.label: Yes, I have it or will obtain it before filing
No, I have not requested it yet
I am not sure my employer will sign it

yes.message: Form T2200 confirmed. Home office and vehicle expense claims remain on your checklist.

document_checklist_add.document: Form T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment, signed by employer

no.message: Request Form T2200 from your employer before filing. Your employer is obligated to complete it if they required you to work from home or use your vehicle. Without it CRA will deny the deduction. We have flagged this as a priority action.

preparer_flag.flag: Form T2200 not yet obtained — client must request from employer before filing. Claim will be denied without it under ITA s.8(10).

unsure.message: If your employer required you to work from home, they are legally obligated to sign Form T2200 when requested. We have flagged this for follow-up with your preparer.

preparer_flag.flag: T2200 status uncertain — confirm employer obligation with client and obtain before filing

screens.title: Did you use your personal vehicle for work purposes in 2025?
screens.instruction: Eligible use includes driving to client sites, job sites, or between work locations. Commuting from home to your regular workplace is not deductible. A Form T2200 from your employer is required under ITA s.8(10).

options.label: Yes — I drove to client sites, job sites, or between work locations

true.message: Motor vehicle expenses are deductible under ITA s.8(1)(h.1) in proportion to employment kilometres driven versus total kilometres. Keep a detailed mileage log — CRA routinely audits vehicle claims and a log is essential to support the deduction.

document_checklist_add.document: Vehicle mileage log for 2025 — date, destination, business purpose, kilometres per trip, total annual kilometres, and total business kilometres

screens.title: Did you pay any of these work-related expenses out of pocket in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. These must be unreimbursed costs you were required to incur as a condition of employment. Each type has specific rules under ITA s.8(1).

options.label: Union dues or professional association dues
Tools or equipment required for work

union_dues.message: Union dues and professional dues are deductible under ITA s.8(1)(i). They should already appear in Box 44 of your T4. If they are not on your T4 bring your receipts or membership statements.

tools.message: Employed tradespersons may deduct the cost of eligible tools under ITA s.8(1)(s), subject to the lesser of $500 or the amount by which tool costs exceed the union dues deduction. Apprentice mechanics may deduct up to $4,000 under ITA s.8(1)(r). Limits apply — your preparer will confirm which rule applies.

supplies.message: Office supplies are deductible under ITA s.8(1)(i)(iii) if your employer required you to pay for them and your employment contract or Form T2200 confirms this obligation.

licensing.message: Annual professional licensing fees required to maintain your occupational licence are deductible under ITA s.8(1)(i)(iv). One-time registration or examination fees do not qualify.

branch_completion_summary.message: Employment income section complete. Here is what we have identified so far.
highlighted_documents: All T4 slips from each employer
Form T2200 signed by employer — if home office or vehicle expenses claimed
Vehicle mileage log — if vehicle expenses claimed

intro: Beyond T4 employment income, many other amounts must be reported on your T1. Some arrive as slips, others you must track yourself. Let's make sure nothing is missed.

screens.title: Did you receive CPP or OAS payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: CPP and OAS are taxable income under ITA s.56(1)(a). You will receive a T4A(P) for CPP and a T4A(OAS) for OAS. These screens appear for taxpayers 65 and older — if you received CPP disability benefits at any age those are covered here too.

true.message: CPP and OAS are taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a). T4A(P) and T4A(OAS) slips are issued by Service Canada by late February. Up to 50% of CPP income may be assigned to a spouse for tax purposes under ITA s.60.03 — your preparer can model whether splitting is beneficial.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A(P) — CPP Statement of Benefits and T4A(OAS) — OAS Statement of Benefits from Service Canada

false.message: No CPP or OAS income noted.

screens.title: Did you receive pension payments from a former employer's registered pension plan in 2025?
screens.instruction: This includes defined benefit (DB) and defined contribution (DC) pension payments. You will receive a T4A slip. This is considered eligible pension income under ITA s.118(3).

true.message: Employer registered pension income is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a) and reported on a T4A slip. It qualifies as eligible pension income for the Pension Income Credit under ITA s.118(3) — worth up to $2,000 federally. Up to 50% may be allocated to a spouse under ITA s.60.03 if both are 65 or older, or if you are receiving pension payments from a registered pension plan at any age.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip — Statement of Pension Retirement Annuity and Other Income from former employer's pension plan

screens.title: Did you receive pension income from another country in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes pensions from a former employer abroad, government pensions from another country, or US Social Security. All foreign pension income is taxable in Canada under ITA s.56(1)(a) and must be converted to Canadian dollars.

true.message: Foreign pension income is taxable in Canada under ITA s.56(1)(a) at the Bank of Canada annual average exchange rate. Tax treaties with the source country may reduce foreign withholding tax, and a Foreign Tax Credit under ITA s.126(1) offsets taxes paid abroad to avoid double taxation. US Social Security is taxable at 85% of benefits received under the Canada-US Tax Treaty.

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain foreign pension statement, confirm currency conversion rate used, and gather proof of foreign tax withheld — applicable tax treaty must be identified

screens.title: Did you receive Employment Insurance (EI) benefits in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes regular EI, maternity, parental, sickness, compassionate care, and fishing benefits. All EI is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a)(iv). You will receive a T4E slip.

true.message: EI benefits are taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a)(iv) and reported on a T4E slip. If your net income exceeds $79,000 in 2025 you may be required to repay a portion under ITA s.145 — this is called the EI social benefits repayment and appears on Schedule 1. If you collected EI in any of the prior 10 years your preparer should verify whether the repayment rule applies.

document_checklist_add.document: T4E — Statement of Employment Insurance and Other Benefits from Service Canada

screens.title: Did you withdraw any funds from an RRSP in 2025?
screens.instruction: Any RRSP withdrawal — other than under the Home Buyers Plan or Lifelong Learning Plan — is fully taxable as income in the year received under ITA s.56(1)(h). Withholding tax is deducted at source but may not cover your full liability.

options.label: Yes — under the Home Buyers Plan (HBP)
Yes — under the Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP)

yes_regular.message: Regular RRSP withdrawals are fully taxable under ITA s.56(1)(h). You will receive a T4RSP slip showing the withdrawal and withholding tax. Note that once withdrawn, RRSP contribution room for that amount is permanently lost — there is no re-contribution room created.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RSP — Statement of RRSP Income showing withdrawal amount and withholding tax deducted

yes_hbp.message: HBP withdrawals up to $35,000 are not taxable when used to purchase a qualifying first home under ITA s.146.01. However, annual repayments to your RRSP are required starting the second calendar year after the first withdrawal year. Missed repayments are added to your income for that year. We will cover this in the First Home Purchase section.

yes_llp.message: LLP withdrawals are not taxable while you are enrolled in qualifying full-time education under ITA s.146.02. Maximum lifetime withdrawal is $20,000 ($10,000 per year). Repayments must begin no later than 10 years after the first withdrawal, or the year after you stop being a full-time student. Missed repayments are added to your income.

screens.title: Did you receive spousal or child support payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: Spousal support received is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(b) if paid periodically under a court order or written agreement. Child support received under agreements after May 1, 1997 is not taxable.

options.label: Yes — both spousal and child support

spousal_only.message: Spousal support received is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(b) when paid periodically pursuant to a court order or written agreement. Lump sum payments are generally not taxable. Bring your separation agreement or court order.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order and records of spousal support payments received in 2025

child_only.message: Child support received under agreements made after May 1, 1997 is not taxable — no income to report. Bring your agreement to confirm the commencement date. Pre-1997 agreements follow different rules.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm child support agreement date — pre-May 1997 agreements are taxable under old inclusion-deduction rules

both.message: Spousal support is taxable, child support is not. Your agreement must specify the breakdown between the two. Bring the full agreement — your preparer will identify the taxable portion.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order clearly showing spousal vs child support breakdown and proof of all payments received

screens.title: Did you receive any investment income outside a registered account in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Exclude amounts earned inside a TFSA, RRSP, or RRIF — those are sheltered from current tax. Outside registered accounts, all investment income is reportable.

options.label: Interest income from bank accounts, GICs, or bonds
Sold investments, property, or cryptocurrency at a gain or loss

interest.message: Interest income is fully taxable in the year earned under ITA s.12(1)(c) — regardless of whether it is received or accrued. T5 slips are issued for interest over $50 per institution. Interest under $50 must still be reported without a slip.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 slips — Statement of Investment Income showing interest earned from each financial institution

canadian_dividends.message: Canadian dividends are taxable but eligible for the Dividend Tax Credit under ITA s.121, which partially offsets the corporate tax already paid. Eligible dividends (from large public corporations) are grossed up 38% — your T5 shows both the actual and grossed-up amounts. Ineligible dividends (from CCPCs) are grossed up 15%.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 slips — Statement of Investment Income showing actual and grossed-up dividend amounts and Dividend Tax Credit

foreign_dividends.message: Foreign dividends are fully taxable as foreign income — there is no gross-up or Dividend Tax Credit available. Foreign withholding tax deducted at source may generate a Foreign Tax Credit under ITA s.126(1). If total foreign property cost exceeded $100,000 at any point in 2025 a T1135 Foreign Income Verification must be filed.

document_checklist_add.document: T5 or NR4 slips for foreign dividends and proof of foreign withholding tax paid

capital_gains.message: Capital gains and losses are reported under ITA s.39(1). For 2025, the inclusion rate is 1/2 for individuals on the first $250,000 of net capital gains, and 2/3 above that threshold. Cryptocurrency dispositions are taxable events — every trade, conversion, or spend is a disposition. Bring trade confirmations or brokerage statements showing Adjusted Cost Base (ACB) and proceeds for each transaction.

document_checklist_add.document: Trade confirmations or brokerage statements showing ACB and proceeds for all dispositions in 2025 — including cryptocurrency transactions

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm ACB for every disposition — particularly cryptocurrency where per-coin tracking is complex and often incomplete

none.message: No investment income outside registered accounts noted.

screens.title: Did you earn rental income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Includes renting a room, basement suite, or entire property. Short-term rentals such as Airbnb also qualify. Rental income is property income under ITA s.9(1), not employment income.

true.message: Rental income is taxable as property income under ITA s.9(1). Eligible rental expenses deductible under ITA s.18(1)(a) include: mortgage interest (not principal), property tax, insurance, repairs and maintenance, utilities paid by you, and a portion of your own home expenses if renting a portion of your principal residence. Note: if you rent part of your principal residence, the change-in-use rules under ITA s.45(1) may affect your future principal residence exemption — discuss with your preparer before filing.

document_checklist_add.document: Rental income and expense records for 2025 — mortgage statement, property tax bill, insurance, receipts for all repairs and eligible expenses

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm if rental property is part of principal residence — partial rental may affect principal residence exemption under ITA s.45(1) on future sale

branch_completion_summary.message: Other income section complete. Here is what we have identified so far.
highlighted_documents: All T4A, T4A(P), T4A(OAS), T4E slips
T5 slips and brokerage statements for investment income
Trade confirmations showing ACB and proceeds for capital gains
Rental income and expense records
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: Life event sections relevant to your 2025 situation.

intro: You indicated you moved in 2025. Depending on the reason for the move and the distance, your moving costs may be deductible under ITA s.62(1). Let's confirm the details.

screens.title: Why did you move?
screens.instruction: Moving expenses are only deductible for an eligible relocation — defined in ITA s.248(1) as a move to enable you to be employed, carry on business, or attend a post-secondary institution at your new location.

options.label: I started a new job or transferred with my employer
I relocated to run or start my own business
I moved to attend a college or university

employment.message: Employment relocation qualifies under ITA s.62(1). One more question to confirm the distance threshold.

business.message: Relocation to carry on business qualifies under ITA s.62(1). One more question to confirm the distance threshold.

post_secondary.message: Students who relocate for full-time post-secondary education may deduct moving expenses under ITA s.62(1) — but only against scholarships, bursaries, or income earned at the new location. One more question to confirm the distance threshold.

other.message: Moving expenses are deductible only for employment, business, or education relocations under ITA s.62(1). A personal relocation — for family, lifestyle, or retirement — does not qualify regardless of distance.

screens.title: Was your new home at least 40 kilometres closer to your new work or school?
screens.instruction: The distance is measured in a straight line from each home to the new work or school location — not driving distance. Under ITA s.248(1), your new home must be at least 40km closer to your new principal place of employment or education than your old home was.

options.label: I am not sure — it might be close

true.message: 40km threshold confirmed. Moving expenses are added to your checklist under ITA s.62(1).

unsure.message: Your preparer can verify the 40km measurement using your old and new addresses. We have kept moving expenses on your checklist as possible — gather your receipts in the meantime.

false.message: The 40km threshold under ITA s.248(1) is not met. Moving expenses are not deductible in this case.

screens.title: Did you move to a different province or territory?
screens.instruction: Your province of residence on December 31, 2025 determines which provincial tax rates and credits apply for the full year — even if you moved mid-year.

options.label: Yes — I moved to a different province or territory
No — I moved within the same province

true.message: Provincial change noted. Your province of residence on December 31 determines provincial tax rates and credits for the entire year. If you moved to or from Ontario, Ontario-specific credits such as the OTB and LIFT Credit are affected.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm exact date of provincial move — determines Ontario credit eligibility and provincial tax rates for the year

false.message: Same-province move noted. Provincial credits are unaffected.

screens.title: Which moving expenses did you incur in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. This builds your document checklist — we are not asking for amounts yet. All eligible moving expense categories are defined in ITA s.62(3).

options.label: Travel costs to the new location — gas, meals, flights, accommodation en route
Lease cancellation costs at old residence
I sold my old home — realtor fees, legal fees, mortgage prepayment penalty
I purchased a new home — legal fees, land transfer taxes
Costs to maintain old home while it sat vacant after I moved

travel.message: Travel costs en route to the new location are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(b). Keep receipts for gas, meals (subject to the 50% meals limitation), and accommodation. Reasonable travel by the most direct route qualifies.

document_checklist_add.document: Travel receipts — gas, meals, and accommodation en route to new location

movers_storage.message: Moving company invoices and storage costs during the relocation are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(a).

document_checklist_add.document: Moving company invoice and storage facility receipts

temp_lodging.message: Temporary lodging while you are settling in at the new location is eligible under ITA s.62(3)(c) for up to 15 days. Meals during this period are also eligible, subject to the 50% limitation.

document_checklist_add.document: Temporary lodging receipts near new location — up to 15 days eligible under ITA s.62(3)(c)

lease_cancellation.message: Lease cancellation fees paid to break your old lease are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(f).

document_checklist_add.document: Lease cancellation agreement and proof of penalty payment

sold_old_home.message: Costs of selling your old home are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(e) — realtor commissions, legal fees, and mortgage prepayment penalties. These are often the largest moving expense amounts.

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from sale of old home — showing realtor commissions, legal fees, and any mortgage penalty

bought_new_home.message: Legal fees and land transfer taxes on your new home are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(d) — but only if you sold your old home or if this is a move for a new job or business.

document_checklist_add.document: Statement of Adjustments from purchase of new home — showing legal fees and land transfer taxes paid

maintain_vacant.message: Costs to maintain a vacant former residence while it is listed for sale are eligible under ITA s.62(3)(f) — including utilities, insurance, and property tax. There is no fixed time limit but costs must be reasonable and the home must have been actively listed.

document_checklist_add.document: Receipts for vacant home maintenance costs — utilities, insurance, property tax while actively listed for sale

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm duration old home was vacant and whether it was actively listed — required to support maintenance cost eligibility under ITA s.62(3)(f)

screens.title: Did you earn employment or business income at your new location in 2025?
screens.instruction: Moving expenses are only deductible against income earned at the new location in the same year under ITA s.62(1). If your income at the new location was insufficient, unused expenses carry forward to the following year under ITA s.62(1)(c).

options.label: Yes — I worked at the new location for most of the year
Yes — but only for a portion of the year after the move
Not yet — I moved late in the year or am still seeking work

full_year.message: Good. Your moving expenses are deductible against your new location income, subject to the total eligible amounts.

partial.message: Your deduction is capped at income earned at the new location in 2025. Any excess moving expenses carry forward automatically to 2026 under ITA s.62(1)(c) and are deductible when you earn sufficient income there.

preparer_flag.flag: Calculate income earned at new location in 2025 to determine deduction ceiling — carry forward remainder to 2026

none_yet.message: If no income was earned at the new location in 2025, the full amount of eligible moving expenses carries forward to 2026 under ITA s.62(1)(c). No deduction is available in 2025.

preparer_flag.flag: No new location income in 2025 — carry forward full moving expense amount to 2026 return

screens.title: Did you have unclaimed moving expenses carried forward from 2024?
screens.instruction: If you moved in late 2024 and earned insufficient income at the new location that year, unused expenses carry forward to 2025 under ITA s.62(1)(c). Your 2024 Notice of Assessment will show the carryforward amount.

true.message: Prior year moving expense carryforward is deductible in 2025 against income earned at the new location. Your 2024 Notice of Assessment shows the exact amount available.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms moving expense carryforward balance available for 2025

unsure.message: Check your 2024 Notice of Assessment — CRA lists all carryforward amounts there. Bring it to your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — verify whether moving expense carryforward balance exists

false.message: No prior year moving expense carryforward noted.

branch_completion_summary.message: Relocation section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: All moving receipts — travel, movers, storage, and temporary lodging
Statement of Adjustments if home was sold or purchased
2024 Notice of Assessment if a carryforward balance is suspected
Proof of new work location — offer letter, T4, or first pay stub

intro: A change in marital status during 2025 affects several areas of your T1 — credits, deductions, registered plan rules, and how certain income is reported. Let's work through the details.

screens.title: What changed in your marital status in 2025?

options.label: I got married or entered a common-law relationship
I separated from my spouse or common-law partner
My spouse or common-law partner passed away

married_or_commonlaw.message: From the date of marriage or common-law status, your spouse's net income affects the spousal amount credit under ITA s.118(1)(a), GST/HST Credit, Canada Child Benefit, and pension income splitting eligibility. We will cover the key items.

separated.message: Separation has several tax implications — support payment deductibility under ITA s.60(b), adjustment of credits based on December 31 status, the eligible dependant amount under ITA s.118(1)(b) if you have children, and property transfer rules under ITA s.73(1). We will work through each.

divorced.message: Divorce finalization affects support payment deductibility under ITA s.60(b), credit eligibility based on December 31 status, and property dispositions. We will cover each area.

widowed.message: We are sorry for your loss. The death of a spouse triggers specific rules — RRSP/RRIF spousal rollover under ITA s.146(8.1), deemed disposition of capital property under ITA s.70(5), and changes to your own credits. Several of these items have time-sensitive deadlines. We will flag each one.

screens.title: Does your new spouse or common-law partner have net income of their own?
screens.instruction: Your spouse's net income determines your eligibility for the Spousal Amount credit under ITA s.118(1)(a) and affects several income-tested credits and benefits.

options.label: I would prefer not to say

none.message: You may claim the full Spousal Amount credit under ITA s.118(1)(a) — worth up to $2,421 federally in 2025. The credit is reduced dollar-for-dollar by your spouse's net income and fully eliminated once their net income equals the basic personal amount.

low.message: The Spousal Amount credit is reduced dollar-for-dollar by your spouse's net income. A partial credit under ITA s.118(1)(a) likely applies — your preparer will calculate the exact amount.

moderate_or_above.message: At this income level the Spousal Amount credit is fully eliminated. Your spouse's income is still relevant for GST/HST Credit and Canada Child Benefit family income calculations.

prefer_not_to_say.message: We will flag the Spousal Amount credit as possible — your preparer will determine eligibility from your spouse's return.

screens.title: When did your separation occur in 2025?
screens.instruction: The timing of separation affects which credits you can claim and how support payment deductibility is calculated for the year.

early_2025.message: For most of 2025 you were separated. Your December 31 marital status determines credit eligibility — the Spousal Amount does not apply. The Eligible Dependant Amount under ITA s.118(1)(b) may be available if you have a child.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm exact separation date — affects credit eligibility and support payment deductibility calculations for 2025

late_2025.message: You were coupled for most of 2025. The Spousal Amount is based on your December 31 status, so it does not apply for 2025 if separated at year-end. Your preparer will review which credits apply for the full year versus the period together.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial year marital status — confirm exact separation date for credit eligibility and proration

screens.title: Did you make spousal or child support payments in 2025?
screens.instruction: Spousal support paid periodically under a court order or written agreement is deductible under ITA s.60(b). Child support paid under agreements made after May 1, 1997 is not deductible.

options.label: Yes — both spousal and child support

spousal_only.message: Spousal support paid periodically pursuant to a court order or written agreement is deductible under ITA s.60(b). Lump sum payments are generally not deductible. Bring your agreement and proof of payments made.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order specifying support terms and records of all spousal support payments made in 2025

child_only.message: Child support paid under agreements made after May 1, 1997 is not deductible under the current rules — no deduction to claim. Bring your agreement to confirm the commencement date.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm child support agreement commencement date — pre-May 1997 agreements follow the old inclusion-deduction regime

both.message: Spousal support is deductible, child support is not. Your agreement must clearly separate the two amounts. Bring the full agreement — your preparer will identify the deductible portion under ITA s.60(b).

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order clearly showing spousal vs child support breakdown and proof of all payments made in 2025

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm breakdown of spousal vs child support in agreement to determine deductible amount

screens.title: Did you incur legal fees related to your separation or divorce in 2025?
screens.instruction: Legal fees paid to establish or enforce a right to support payments are deductible under ITA s.60(o.1). Legal fees for property settlement or the divorce itself are not deductible.

true.message: Legal fees to establish or enforce the right to support payments are deductible under ITA s.60(o.1). Fees for property division, custody arrangements, or the divorce proceedings themselves are not deductible. Bring itemized legal invoices — your preparer will identify the deductible portion.

document_checklist_add.document: Legal invoices — itemized billing showing fees for support enforcement vs property or divorce matters

screens.title: Did you transfer property to or receive property from your spouse as part of the separation?
screens.instruction: Property transfers between spouses on marriage breakdown follow specific rollover rules under ITA s.73(1) — different from arm's length transfers.

options.label: I transferred property to my spouse
I received property from my spouse

transferred_to_spouse.message: Property transferred to a spouse on marriage breakdown is deemed disposed at its adjusted cost base under ITA s.73(1) — no immediate capital gain is triggered. The receiving spouse inherits your cost base. Your preparer must document the transfer carefully to establish the ACB that carries over.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents for all transferred property — establishes the ACB that rolls over to your spouse under ITA s.73(1)

preparer_flag.flag: Document ACB of all transferred property — s.73(1) rollover defers gain but ACB must be recorded for the receiving spouse

received_from_spouse.message: Property received from a spouse on marriage breakdown carries over at the transferor's adjusted cost base under ITA s.73(1). Your future capital gain is calculated from their original cost — not the fair market value at the time of transfer. Obtain original purchase documentation from your spouse.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents from transferring spouse — confirms inherited ACB for future capital gains calculation

preparer_flag.flag: Obtain ACB documentation from transferring spouse — determines your future capital gain when property is eventually sold

both.message: Multiple property transfers noted in both directions. Each transfer must be documented individually with the original cost base. Professional review is strongly recommended before these transactions are reported.

preparer_flag.flag: Multiple property transfers on marriage breakdown — document ACB for each separately. Professional review required.

screens.title: Did your spouse have an RRSP or RRIF at the time of their passing?

options.label: Yes — both an RRSP and a RRIF

rrsp.message: If you are the named beneficiary of your spouse's RRSP you may transfer the proceeds directly to your own RRSP or RRIF on a tax-deferred basis under ITA s.146(8.1) — no income inclusion in either return. Without this rollover election, the full RRSP value is included in your spouse's terminal return. This election must be made before the estate is settled. Act urgently.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP account statement at date of death, beneficiary designation form, and death certificate — required to initiate spousal rollover under ITA s.146(8.1)

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: Spousal RRSP rollover election must be made before estate is settled — confirm beneficiary designation immediately and file election with financial institution

rrif.message: RRIF proceeds paid to a surviving spouse who is a named beneficiary may be transferred to your own RRSP or RRIF tax-deferred under ITA s.146.3. Minimum withdrawal rules apply going forward from your own plan. Bring the RRIF statements and beneficiary designation documents.

document_checklist_add.document: RRIF account statement at date of death, beneficiary designation form, and death certificate

preparer_flag.flag: Spousal RRIF rollover — confirm beneficiary designation, initiate transfer, and calculate minimum withdrawal obligations from the new plan

both.message: Both RRSP and RRIF noted. Each requires a separate rollover election at the financial institution holding the plan. Both elections must be completed before the estate is settled. Act immediately.

document_checklist_add.document: Statements for both RRSP and RRIF at date of death, beneficiary designations for each plan, and death certificate

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: Both RRSP and RRIF rollover elections required — each must be completed at the holding institution before estate settlement

false.message: No RRSP or RRIF noted for deceased spouse.

unsure.message: Contact the financial institutions your spouse dealt with urgently to confirm whether any RRSP or RRIF existed. The rollover window closes when the estate is settled — missing it means the full plan value is taxed in the terminal return.

preparer_flag.flag: URGENT: Confirm whether deceased spouse held an RRSP or RRIF — rollover opportunity is time-limited and irreversible if missed

screens.title: Did you inherit capital property from your spouse — such as a home, investments, or rental property?

true.message: Capital property transferred to a surviving spouse on death is deemed disposed at its adjusted cost base under the spousal rollover in ITA s.70(6) — deferring any capital gain until you eventually sell. You inherit the deceased's original cost base, not the fair market value at death. Obtain original purchase documents for all inherited property. This documentation will be essential when you sell.

document_checklist_add.document: Original purchase documents for all capital property inherited from spouse — establishes the ACB that carries over to you under ITA s.70(6)

preparer_flag.flag: Document ACB of all inherited capital property — spousal rollover under ITA s.70(6) defers the gain but the ACB must be on file for the future sale

branch_completion_summary.message: Marital status section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: Separation agreement or court order — if separated or divorced
Proof of support payments made or received in 2025
Itemized legal invoices — if deducting legal fees under ITA s.60(o.1)
Original purchase documents for any transferred or inherited capital property
RRSP/RRIF statements and beneficiary designation forms — if spouse passed away

intro: Children and other dependents unlock several deductions and credits. Let's make sure you claim everything you're entitled to — these are among the most commonly missed items, especially for newcomers and first-time parents.

screens.title: Which of the following describes your children or dependents in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. This determines which deductions and credits are available to you.

options.label: Child under 7 years old at December 31, 2025
Child of any age with a physical or mental disability
Adult dependent — parent, grandparent, or other relative I support financially

child_under_7.message: Children under 7 attract the highest child care deduction limit — $8,000 per child under ITA s.63(1). We will confirm eligibility in the next questions.

child_7_to_16.message: Children aged 7 to 16 attract a child care deduction limit of $5,000 per child under ITA s.63(1). We will confirm eligibility in the next questions.

child_with_disability.message: A child with a disability qualifies for an $11,000 child care limit under ITA s.63(1) and may also be eligible for the Disability Tax Credit transfer to you as the supporting parent under ITA s.118.3(2). We will cover both.

adult_dependent.message: Supporting an adult dependent may qualify you for the Canada Caregiver Amount under ITA s.118(1)(c.1) or the Infirm Dependent credit under ITA s.118(1)(d). We will confirm the details.

screens.title: Are you a single parent — supporting a child without a cohabiting spouse or common-law partner?
screens.instruction: Single parents may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount for one qualifying child — worth up to $2,421 federally in 2025 under ITA s.118(1)(b).

options.label: Yes — I am the primary supporting parent without a cohabiting partner
No — the other parent or a partner is also in the household

true.message: You may claim the Eligible Dependant Amount under ITA s.118(1)(b) for one child — this gives a federal credit equivalent to the spousal amount. Important: if you paid child support for this child in 2025 you cannot claim this credit for the same child in the same year.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm no child support was paid for the same child claimed as eligible dependant — ITA s.118(1)(b) and s.118(5) prohibit claiming both

false.message: Eligible Dependant Amount does not apply when a cohabiting partner is present.

screens.title: Did you pay for child care in 2025?
screens.instruction: Eligible child care includes daycare, licensed home care, babysitting, nanny, boarding school, and overnight camps. The child must be 16 or under at any time in the year, or have a disability.

true.message: Child care expenses are deductible under ITA s.63(1) by the lower-income spouse. Annual limits: $8,000 per child under 7, $5,000 per child aged 7–16, and $11,000 for a child with a disability. The deduction cannot exceed 2/3 of the claimant's earned income for the year.

screens.title: Do you have receipts from your child care provider showing their name, address, and SIN or business number?
screens.instruction: CRA requires provider identification to support the child care deduction under ITA s.63(1). Without it the claim can be denied on audit.

options.label: Yes — I have proper receipts with all required information
Some receipts — missing SIN or business number

true.message: Receipts confirmed. Child care deduction is well supported.

document_checklist_add.document: Child care receipts — provider name, address, SIN or business number, and amounts paid per child

partial.message: Request the missing SIN or business number from your provider before filing. CRA frequently audits child care claims and will disallow unsupported amounts.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care receipts incomplete — obtain provider SIN or business number before filing

false.message: Without receipts the child care deduction cannot be supported on audit. Contact your provider immediately. We have flagged this as urgent.

preparer_flag.flag: No child care receipts — obtain from provider urgently. Deduction unsupportable without receipts showing provider SIN or business number.

screens.title: Between you and your spouse or partner, who had the lower net income in 2025?
screens.instruction: Child care expenses must be claimed by the lower-income spouse under ITA s.63(1). There are limited exceptions when the higher-income spouse may claim — for example, when the lower-income spouse was a student or in prison.

options.label: I did — I am the lower-income spouse
My spouse or partner had lower income
Our incomes were similar — not sure who was lower

self.message: You claim the child care deduction on your own return.

spouse.message: Your spouse claims the child care deduction on their return. Share the receipts with their preparer as well.

preparer_flag.flag: Child care deduction claimed by spouse — confirm lower-income status and provide receipts to spouse's preparer

similar.message: When incomes are close your preparer will confirm which spouse had lower net income based on final figures and allocate the deduction accordingly.

preparer_flag.flag: Incomes similar — preparer to confirm lower-income spouse and optimize child care deduction allocation

screens.title: Do you have or are you considering a Registered Education Savings Plan (RESP) for your child?
screens.instruction: An RESP grows tax-free and attracts the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 annually — a free $500 per year per child from the federal government.

options.label: Yes — I contributed to an existing RESP in 2025
I opened a new RESP in 2025
I do not have one yet but I am interested

existing.message: RESP contributions attract the CESG of 20% on contributions up to $2,500 per year under ITA s.146.1 — maximum $500 grant annually per beneficiary, $7,200 lifetime. Contributions are not tax deductible but investment growth is tax-sheltered. Withdrawals are taxed in the child's hands.

document_checklist_add.document: RESP annual statement from financial institution showing contributions and CESG received

new.message: New RESP noted. Ensure the CESG application is submitted through your financial institution — it does not happen automatically. The child must be a Canadian resident under 18 to receive the grant. Contributions made in the year of opening are eligible for the grant.

preparer_flag.flag: New RESP opened — confirm CESG application submitted and child meets residency requirement

considering.message: An RESP is worth opening even with a small contribution — you only need $2,500 to maximize the $500 annual CESG. Unused grant room from prior years accumulates and can be caught up at up to $1,000 in grants per year. For newcomers: CESG eligibility begins from the date your child becomes a Canadian resident.

preparer_flag.flag: RESP not yet opened — advise on CESG catch-up opportunity and optimal timing for contributions

screens.title: Does your child have a Disability Tax Credit (DTC) certificate approved by CRA?
screens.instruction: The DTC requires a certified Form T2201 on file with CRA. If your child cannot use the full disability amount, the unused portion transfers to you as the supporting parent under ITA s.118.3(2).

options.label: Yes — Form T2201 has been approved by CRA

true.message: DTC certificate confirmed. If your child cannot use the full disability amount against their own income, the unused portion transfers to you under ITA s.118.3(2). Your preparer will calculate the transferable amount. The $11,000 child care limit also applies.

document_checklist_add.document: CRA DTC approval letter for dependent — confirms Form T2201 approval date and period covered

applied.message: DTC application pending. If approved before you file, the credit applies from the certificate start date. File your return on time — if approval comes after filing, amend the return for the applicable years (up to 10 years retroactively).

preparer_flag.flag: DTC application pending for dependent — monitor CRA approval. File return on time and amend if approved after filing. Retroactive up to 10 years.

false.message: If your child has a prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts basic activities of daily living, they may qualify for the DTC. A medical practitioner must certify Form T2201. CRA approval is retroactive up to 10 years — this is worth pursuing if there is a qualifying condition.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC not applied for — assess child's eligibility with medical practitioner. Retroactive claim possible up to 10 years if approved.

screens.title: Are you currently receiving the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) for your child?
screens.instruction: The CCB is a tax-free monthly benefit for families with children under 18. It is not reported as income but must be applied for and is recalculated annually based on your prior year net income.

options.label: I had a new child or arrived in Canada in 2025 and have not yet applied

true.message: CCB is not taxable income. However, it is recalculated each July based on prior year net income — filing your T1 return on time is essential. Late or non-filing can interrupt CCB payments.

preparer_flag.flag: File return on time — CCB recalculated July based on prior year net income. Late filing interrupts payments.

newly_eligible.message: Apply for the CCB immediately through CRA My Account or by filing Form RC66. Newcomers must also file Form RC66SCH to confirm residency status. CCB payments are not retroactive beyond 11 months — apply now to preserve your entitlement.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not yet applied for — complete RC66 (and RC66SCH for newcomers) immediately. Payments not retroactive beyond 11 months.

false.message: If you have children under 18 and are not receiving CCB, apply immediately — there may be retroactive entitlement up to 11 months. Your preparer can assist.

preparer_flag.flag: CCB not being received — confirm eligibility and apply. Retroactive entitlement up to 11 months.

screens.title: Did you provide a home for an elderly parent, grandparent, or infirm adult relative in 2025?

options.label: Yes — they lived in my home
Yes — I supported them financially but they lived elsewhere

lived_with_me.message: You may be eligible for the Canada Caregiver Amount under ITA s.118(1)(c.1) for an infirm dependent living with you. The federal credit is up to $7,999 for 2025, reduced by the dependent's net income above $17,670. Medical documentation of infirmity may be required by CRA.

document_checklist_add.document: Dependent's net income information — required to calculate Canada Caregiver Amount phase-out under ITA s.118(1)(c.1)

preparer_flag.flag: Caregiver amount — confirm dependent's net income and infirmity status. Medical documentation may be required by CRA.

financial_support.message: Financial support of an infirm dependent living elsewhere may qualify for the Infirm Dependent credit under ITA s.118(1)(d). The dependent must have a physical or mental infirmity and be dependent on you due to that infirmity. Your preparer will confirm eligibility.

preparer_flag.flag: Infirm dependent living elsewhere — confirm infirmity status and degree of financial dependence for ITA s.118(1)(d) credit

screens.title: Did you transfer money or property to a child under 18 to invest in their name in 2025?
screens.instruction: Attribution rules under ITA s.74.1(2) apply income earned on transferred property back to you until the year the child turns 18. Capital gains are exempt from attribution — only income such as interest and dividends is attributed.

true.message: Attribution rules under ITA s.74.1(2) require you to report income (interest, dividends) earned on funds transferred to a minor child on your own return until the year they turn 18. Capital gains earned in the child's hands are not attributed back. Your preparer needs the amount transferred and any income earned on it.

preparer_flag.flag: Income attribution applies — report income earned on funds transferred to minor on your return under ITA s.74.1(2). Capital gains not attributed.

branch_completion_summary.message: Children and dependents section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: Child care receipts — provider name, SIN or business number, amounts paid per child
CRA DTC approval letter if disability credit applies to a dependent
RESP annual contribution statement
Dependent's net income information if caregiver credit is claimed
Form RC66 and RC66SCH if CCB not yet applied for

intro: Self-employment income is reported on Form T2125 — you report gross revenue and deduct eligible business expenses, paying tax only on net income. Let's make sure all legitimate expenses are captured and key obligations are flagged.

screens.title: Which of the following describes your self-employment in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Different business structures have different reporting requirements on your T1.

options.label: Sole proprietor — operating under my own name or a business name
Partnership — sharing the business with one or more partners
Freelance or gig work — contracts, platforms, or occasional income
Professional practice — legal, medical, accounting, or consulting

sole_proprietor.message: Sole proprietorship income and expenses are reported on Form T2125 — Statement of Business or Professional Activities. Net income flows directly to Line 13500 of your T1.

partnership.message: Your share of partnership income and expenses is reported on Form T2125. A T5013 Partnership Information Return may also be issued. Bring both your T5013 slip and the partnership agreement showing your profit-sharing percentage.

document_checklist_add.document: T5013 Partnership slip (if issued) and partnership agreement showing your profit/loss sharing percentage

incorporated.message: Your corporation files its own T2 return separately. On your personal T1 you report: salary drawn from the corporation (T4 slip), dividends received (T5 slip), and any taxable shareholder benefits. This tool covers your personal T1 only — the T2 corporate return requires separate preparation.

preparer_flag.flag: Corporation requires separate T2 return — not covered by this tool. Confirm T4 salary and T5 dividend slips from corporation for personal T1.

freelance_gig.message: Freelance and gig income — including Uber, DoorDash, Airbnb hosting, Fiverr, or contract work — is fully taxable as business income under ITA s.9(1). All eligible expenses directly related to earning that income are deductible on Form T2125.

professional.message: Professional practice income is reported on Form T2125. Note: certain designated professionals (lawyers, accountants, engineers, physicians, chiropractors, dentists) may elect to exclude unbilled work-in-progress from income under ITA s.34. Your preparer will advise whether this election applies.

preparer_flag.flag: Professional practice — confirm ITA s.34 work-in-progress election status if applicable to this profession

screens.title: Approximately what was your gross business revenue in 2025?
screens.instruction: Gross revenue before any expenses. This determines GST/HST registration requirements under the Excise Tax Act.

options.label: I would prefer not to say

under_30k.message: Under $30,000 cumulative gross revenue you qualify as a small supplier under the Excise Tax Act — GST/HST registration is not mandatory. However voluntary registration allows you to claim Input Tax Credits (ITCs) on your business purchases. Your preparer can advise whether voluntary registration benefits you.

preparer_flag.flag: Revenue under $30,000 — advise on voluntary GST/HST registration and input tax credit opportunity

30k_to_100k.message: Once cumulative revenue exceeds $30,000 in any 12-month period, GST/HST registration is mandatory under the Excise Tax Act. If not already registered this must be done immediately — penalties apply for late registration and unremitted GST. The Quick Method of accounting is available and may simplify compliance.

preparer_flag.flag: GST/HST registration mandatory above $30,000 — confirm registration status. Penalties apply for late registration.

100k_to_500k.message: GST/HST registration is mandatory. At this revenue level filing is likely quarterly. Input Tax Credits (ITCs) on business expenses are recoverable. Bring your GST/HST returns filed in 2025 and all remittance records.

document_checklist_add.document: GST/HST returns filed in 2025 and remittance payment confirmations

over_500k.message: At this revenue level monthly GST/HST filing is mandatory. Payroll source deductions also apply if you have employees or pay yourself a salary. Bring all GST/HST returns, remittance records, and T4 summaries.

document_checklist_add.document: GST/HST returns and remittances for all 2025 periods plus T4 Summary if employees

prefer_not_to_say.message: We will flag GST/HST registration as a priority item for your preparer to confirm based on your actual revenue.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm gross revenue and GST/HST registration status — mandatory above $30,000 cumulative in any 12-month period

screens.title: How did you track your business income and expenses in 2025?
screens.instruction: CRA requires you to maintain adequate books and records to support all revenue and expense claims on Form T2125.

options.label: Accounting software — QuickBooks, Wave, FreshBooks, or similar
Manual records — paper receipts and notes
I did not keep formal records

accounting_software.message: Good recordkeeping. Export a profit and loss summary for the full year 2025 and bring it to your preparer along with bank statements for reconciliation.

document_checklist_add.document: Profit and loss report from accounting software — full year 2025, plus business bank statements

spreadsheet.message: Spreadsheet records are acceptable. Bring a summary of all income and expenses by category along with supporting receipts. Your preparer will map these to the T2125 expense categories.

document_checklist_add.document: Business income and expense summary spreadsheet plus all supporting receipts organized by category

manual.message: Manual records are acceptable but carry higher audit risk. Organize all receipts by expense category before your preparer appointment. Your preparer will total each category and reconcile against your bank deposits.

document_checklist_add.document: All business receipts organized by expense category plus business bank statements showing all deposits

preparer_flag.flag: Manual records only — organize receipts by category before appointment. Recommend accounting software for 2026 to reduce audit risk.

none.message: Without records CRA can disallow all expense claims on audit and estimate income from bank deposits. Before your appointment gather: all 2025 bank statements, credit card statements, and any receipts you can locate. Your preparer will reconstruct records from bank data. This is a critical risk item.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: No business records kept — gather all bank and credit card statements immediately. Preparer must reconstruct from bank data. High audit risk if reviewed.

document_checklist_add.document: All 2025 bank statements and credit card statements — complete year, all accounts used for business income and expenses

screens.title: Did you use part of your home as your principal place of business in 2025?
screens.instruction: Self-employed individuals may deduct a proportionate share of home expenses under ITA s.18(12). The workspace must be either your principal place of business, or used exclusively for business and regularly for meeting clients.

options.label: Yes — I work primarily from home
Yes — partially, I also have an outside office or client location

true.message: Home office expenses are deductible under ITA s.18(12) based on the proportion of your home used exclusively for business — calculated as business-use square footage divided by total home square footage. Eligible expenses include rent or mortgage interest, property taxes, utilities, heat, and home insurance. The deduction cannot exceed your net business income for the year — excess amounts carry forward.

document_checklist_add.document: Home office receipts for 2025 — rent or mortgage statement, property tax bill, utility bills, insurance, and square footage measurements of business vs total home space

partial.message: A partial home office may still qualify under ITA s.18(12) if it is your principal place of business or is used exclusively for business and regularly for client meetings. Your preparer will determine eligibility based on the actual usage pattern.

preparer_flag.flag: Partial home office — confirm whether workspace qualifies as principal place of business or exclusive business use under ITA s.18(12)

screens.title: Did you use your personal vehicle for business purposes in 2025?
screens.instruction: Business-use vehicle expenses are deductible under ITA s.18(1)(a) in proportion to business kilometres driven. Commuting from home to a fixed office location does not qualify.

options.label: Yes — I drove to client locations, job sites, or for business errands

true.message: Vehicle expenses are deductible under ITA s.18(1)(a) in proportion to business kilometres over total kilometres driven. Eligible expenses include fuel, insurance, repairs, licence fees, and a Capital Cost Allowance (CCA) claim for the vehicle itself. A detailed mileage log is essential — CRA routinely audits vehicle claims and will disallow the deduction without one.

document_checklist_add.document: Mileage log for 2025 — date, destination, business purpose, kilometres per trip, total annual km, and total business km

preparer_flag.flag: Self-employment vehicle claim — confirm CCA class for vehicle, confirm ownership vs lease, and obtain all vehicle operating cost receipts

screens.title: Did you pay any employees or contractors in 2025?
screens.instruction: Employees require payroll source deductions (CPP, EI, income tax) remitted to CRA and T4 slips by February 28. Contractors are paid gross with no deductions — but the distinction is not always clear.

options.label: Yes — employees (I deducted CPP, EI, and income tax from their pay)
Yes — contractors or subcontractors only (no source deductions)

employees.message: Employer payroll obligations: remit CPP (employee and employer portions), EI (employee and 1.4x employer portion), and income tax source deductions to CRA. Issue T4 slips by February 28, 2026. File T4 Summary with CRA. Employer CPP contributions are deductible business expenses.

document_checklist_add.document: Payroll records for all employees — T4 slips issued, remittance receipts, and PD7A payroll remittance statements

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm T4 slips issued by Feb 28 and all 2025 payroll remittances to CRA are up to date

contractors.message: Contractors are paid gross with no source deductions. However CRA may reclassify workers as employees based on control, ownership of tools, and financial risk — this is called worker misclassification and carries significant penalties. If any contractor was paid over $500 in the year, a T4A slip must be issued.

document_checklist_add.document: Contractor invoices and T4A slips issued for contractors paid over $500 in 2025

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm worker classification — CRA may reclassify contractors as employees. T4A required for any contractor paid over $500.

both.message: Both employees and contractors noted. T4 slips required for employees (due Feb 28), T4A slips required for contractors paid over $500. Payroll remittances for employees must be current.

document_checklist_add.document: T4 slips for employees, T4A slips for contractors over $500, payroll remittance records

screens.title: Are you aware that self-employed individuals pay both the employee and employer portions of CPP?
screens.instruction: Self-employed individuals pay CPP contributions on net self-employment income under ITA s.4(1) of the CPP Act — both the employee and employer portions. For 2025 this is approximately 11.4% on net income between $3,500 and $73,200.

options.label: Yes — I am aware and have set funds aside
No — I did not know about this

true.message: Good. The employer portion of your CPP contributions (approximately half) is deductible as a business expense on Line 22200 of your T1. The employee portion is claimed as a credit on Schedule 8.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm CPP contributions on Schedule 8 — deduct employer portion as business expense on Line 22200

false.message: Important: self-employed individuals pay CPP at approximately 11.4% on net self-employment income between $3,500 and $73,200 in 2025 — roughly $7,970 at maximum. This is separate from your income tax and is often a surprise at filing. Set funds aside if possible before your return is filed. The employer portion is deductible as a business expense.

preparer_flag.flag: Client unaware of self-employed CPP obligations — advise on amount owing and employer deduction. Manage cash flow expectations.

branch_completion_summary.message: Self-employment section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: Complete income and expense records — accounting software report, spreadsheet, or organized receipts
Business bank statements for the full year
Mileage log if vehicle expenses claimed
Home office measurements and expense receipts if home office claimed
GST/HST returns and remittance records if registered
Payroll and T4/T4A records if workers were engaged

intro: Retirement income has special rules — pension splitting, RRIF minimum withdrawals, the Age Amount, and OAS repayment thresholds. Let's make sure your retirement picture is fully captured.

screens.title: Which retirement income sources did you receive in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Each source has specific reporting requirements and potential planning opportunities.

options.label: Canada Pension Plan (CPP) or CPP2 benefits
Employer registered pension plan (DB or DC)

cpp.message: CPP benefits are taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a). A T4A(P) slip is issued by Service Canada. Up to 50% of CPP income may be assigned to a spouse under the CPP Act's pension sharing provisions — different from the ITA s.60.03 pension income splitting election. Your preparer can model both options.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A(P) — Statement of CPP Benefits from Service Canada

oas.message: OAS is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a). A T4A(OAS) slip is issued. If your net income exceeds $90,997 in 2025 you are required to repay a portion of OAS under ITA s.180.2 — the OAS clawback. The clawback rate is 15% of net income above the threshold up to the full OAS amount.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A(OAS) — Statement of OAS Benefits from Service Canada

employer_pension.message: Registered pension plan income is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a) and reported on a T4A. It qualifies as eligible pension income for the Pension Income Credit under ITA s.118(3) and for pension income splitting under ITA s.60.03.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip — Statement of Pension Retirement Annuity and Other Income from pension plan

rrif_withdrawal.message: RRIF withdrawals are fully taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a.1). A T4RIF slip is issued. Minimum withdrawals are required each year based on your age — withdrawals above the minimum also qualify as eligible pension income for the Pension Income Credit if you are 65 or older.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RIF — Statement of Income from a Registered Retirement Income Fund

annuity.message: Annuity payments purchased from an RRSP or RRIF are fully taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a.1) and reported on a T4A. Annuity payments from life insurance or non-registered sources may have a partial tax-free component — your preparer will determine the prescribed portion.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip for annuity income — confirm whether annuity is from registered or non-registered source

retiring_allowance.message: A retiring allowance is taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a.ii) and reported on a T4A. You may be able to transfer a portion to your RRSP tax-free — $2,000 per year of pre-1996 service under ITA s.60(j.1). This is an important planning opportunity if any pre-1996 service exists.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm years of pre-1996 service for retiring allowance RRSP transfer under ITA s.60(j.1) — $2,000 per eligible year

screens.title: Do you have a spouse or common-law partner, and are you considering splitting your pension income?
screens.instruction: Pension income splitting under ITA s.60.03 allows you to allocate up to 50% of eligible pension income to a lower-income spouse. This can significantly reduce combined family tax by moving income from a higher bracket to a lower one.

options.label: Yes — I have a spouse and want to explore pension splitting

true.message: Pension income splitting under ITA s.60.03 is made jointly on Form T1032. Eligible pension income includes registered pension plan payments, RRIF withdrawals (if 65 or older), and annuity payments from a registered plan. CPP income is assigned separately through Service Canada, not ITA s.60.03. Your preparer will model the optimal split percentage based on both spouses' income.

preparer_flag.flag: Model optimal pension income split percentage — requires both spouses' income projections. File Form T1032 jointly.

screens.title: Did you turn 65 or older in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Age Amount credit under ITA s.118(2) is available to Canadians 65 or older. For 2025 the maximum federal credit is $8,790, reduced by 15% of net income above $42,335 and fully eliminated above $101,135.

options.label: Yes — I was 65 or older on December 31, 2025

true.message: The Age Amount credit under ITA s.118(2) will be calculated on your return. If your net income exceeds $42,335 the credit phases out at 15 cents per dollar of excess income. At income above $101,135 the credit is fully eliminated. Your preparer will calculate the exact credit based on your final net income.

screens.title: Was your net income in 2025 above approximately $90,000?
screens.instruction: The OAS Recovery Tax under ITA s.180.2 requires repayment of 15% of OAS for every dollar of net income above $90,997 in 2025. At approximately $148,450 the full OAS amount is recovered.

options.label: Yes — my income was likely above $90,000

true.message: OAS Recovery Tax may apply. The clawback rate is 15% of net income above $90,997 under ITA s.180.2. This appears on Schedule 1 and reduces your net OAS. CRA may also request that your monthly OAS payments be reduced prospectively through voluntary tax deductions at source. Your preparer will calculate the exact amount.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS clawback may apply — calculate OAS Recovery Tax under ITA s.180.2. Consider voluntary withholding at source to avoid large year-end balance owing.

false.message: OAS clawback threshold not reached. No recovery tax expected.

unsure.message: We have flagged the OAS clawback as possible — your preparer will confirm based on your actual net income.

screens.title: Did you turn 71 in 2025 and convert your RRSP to a RRIF or annuity?
screens.instruction: Under ITA s.146(2)(b.4), an RRSP must be converted by December 31 of the year you turn 71. Failure to convert results in the full RRSP balance being included in income in the following year.

options.label: Yes — I converted my RRSP to a RRIF or annuity before December 31, 2025
I am not sure if this has been done

true.message: RRIF conversion confirmed. Minimum annual withdrawals are now required based on your age each January 1. Your financial institution calculates the minimum. Withdrawals in excess of the minimum are also taxable but may qualify for the Pension Income Credit if you are 65 or older.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm RRIF minimum withdrawal was taken in 2025 — failure to take minimum results in penalty tax under ITA s.146.3(1)

false.message: CRITICAL: If your RRSP was not converted by December 31 of the year you turned 71, the full remaining RRSP balance is included in your income in the following year under ITA s.146(2)(b.4). Contact your financial institution immediately to initiate conversion and speak with your preparer about remediation options.

preparer_flag.flag: CRITICAL: RRSP not converted by age 71 deadline — full balance potentially includable in income. Contact financial institution immediately and advise preparer of remediation options.

unsure.message: Confirm urgently with your financial institution whether your RRSP has been converted. The conversion deadline is December 31 of the year you turn 71 under ITA s.146(2)(b.4). This is a critical and irreversible item.

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP conversion status unknown — client must confirm with financial institution immediately. Deadline is Dec 31 of age-71 year.

branch_completion_summary.message: Retirement section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: T4A(P) — CPP Statement of Benefits
T4A(OAS) — OAS Statement of Benefits
T4A slip — registered pension plan payments
T4RIF — RRIF withdrawal statement
Form T1032 — joint election for pension income splitting (if applicable)

intro: Post-secondary education creates several tax opportunities — the Tuition Tax Credit, Canada Training Credit, student loan interest, and registered plan interactions. Let's make sure all education-related items are captured.

screens.title: Who attended post-secondary education in 2025?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Tuition credits can be used by the student, transferred to a supporting parent or grandparent, or carried forward.

options.label: I attended a qualifying post-secondary program
My spouse or common-law partner attended

self.message: As a student you may claim the Tuition Tax Credit under ITA s.118.5, the Canada Training Credit under ITA s.122.91, and student loan interest under ITA s.118.62. Unused tuition amounts carry forward indefinitely or may be transferred up to $5,000 to a supporting parent or grandparent under ITA s.118.9.

spouse.message: Your spouse's tuition credit can be transferred to you if your spouse does not need the full amount to reduce their own tax to zero. The maximum transfer is $5,000 under ITA s.118.8. Your spouse must designate the transfer on their return.

child.message: Your child or grandchild may transfer up to $5,000 of unused tuition credit to you under ITA s.118.9, after using enough to reduce their own tax to zero.
