They must designate you on their return (Form T2202). The child can alternatively carry the unused amount forward to their own future returns.
child.activates_flag_note: tuition_transfer_decision flag also relevant — preparer to model transfer vs carryforward

screens.title: Has your educational institution issued a T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate?
screens.instruction: Form T2202 is issued by qualifying Canadian post-secondary institutions for tuition fees paid. It is now filed directly with CRA by the institution — you can access it through CRA My Account.

options.label: Yes — I have or can access my T2202
No — I have not received it yet
I am not sure — this was my first year at this institution

true.message: T2202 confirmed. This form shows eligible tuition fees and months of full-time and part-time enrolment. Both figures are used to calculate your Tuition Tax Credit under ITA s.118.5 and your Canada Training Credit accumulation under ITA s.122.91.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate from post-secondary institution (available in CRA My Account)

false.message: T2202 forms are typically available through CRA My Account by late February. Contact your institution's registrar if it is not available by then.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate — access through CRA My Account or request from institution registrar

unsure.message: Check CRA My Account — institutions are now required to file T2202 directly with CRA. If not available there, contact your institution's registrar.

document_checklist_add.document: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate — check CRA My Account

screens.title: Do you have unused tuition credits carried forward from previous years?
screens.instruction: Unused tuition amounts from prior years accumulate indefinitely and appear on your most recent Notice of Assessment. They cannot be transferred to a supporting parent — only the current year amount can be transferred.

options.label: Yes — I have a carryforward balance

true.message: Prior year tuition carryforward is used in the current year before the current year's tuition credit under ITA s.118.61. Bring your most recent Notice of Assessment which shows the exact carryforward balance.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — shows unused tuition credit carryforward balance

unsure.message: Check your most recent CRA Notice of Assessment or CRA My Account — unused tuition credits appear there. Bring it to your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: Most recent Notice of Assessment — verify unused tuition credit carryforward balance

screens.title: Do you want to transfer your unused 2025 tuition credit to a supporting parent or grandparent?
screens.instruction: You can transfer up to $5,000 of your current year tuition credit to a parent or grandparent under ITA s.118.9 — but only after applying enough to reduce your own tax to zero. Once transferred it cannot be reclaimed as a carryforward.

options.label: Yes — I want to transfer the unused amount to a supporting parent or grandparent
No — I prefer to carry it forward to my own future returns
I want my preparer to model which option is better

true.message: Tuition transfer confirmed. The parent or grandparent will claim the transferred amount. You must designate the specific person on your return by completing the tuition section of Form T2202. The maximum transferable is $5,000 of current year tuition — carryforward amounts from prior years cannot be transferred.

preparer_flag.flag: Tuition transfer elected — confirm receiving person and complete T2202 designation. Transferred amount cannot be reclaimed as carryforward.

false.message: Carryforward confirmed. Unused tuition amounts carry forward indefinitely to your own future returns under ITA s.118.61 and are applied before current year tuition.

discuss.message: Your preparer will model both options based on the parent's and your own projected income to determine which produces the greater total tax saving.

preparer_flag.flag: Model tuition transfer vs carryforward — compare tax saving for student vs supporting parent based on respective marginal rates

screens.title: Are you between 26 and 65 years old and did you pay tuition to an eligible institution in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Canada Training Credit under ITA s.122.91 is a refundable credit of up to $250 per year for eligible Canadians aged 26–65. You accumulate $10 per year in a notional account, visible on your Notice of Assessment. You can claim up to the lesser of the account balance and 50% of tuition paid.

options.label: Yes — I am between 26 and 65 and paid eligible tuition
No — I am under 26 or over 65, or this was an ineligible program

true.message: The Canada Training Credit under ITA s.122.91 is refundable — you receive the credit even if you owe no tax. The maximum is $250 per year (50% of up to $500 in eligible tuition). Check your Notice of Assessment for your Canada Training Credit limit. Any amount claimed reduces your Tuition Tax Credit for the year.

document_checklist_add.document: Notice of Assessment — confirms your Canada Training Credit limit balance for 2025

false.message: Canada Training Credit does not apply.

screens.title: Did you pay interest on a student loan in 2025?
screens.instruction: Interest paid on government student loans (Canada Student Loans, provincial student loans) is eligible for a non-refundable credit under ITA s.118.62. Private bank loans and lines of credit do not qualify.

options.label: Yes — on a government student loan
Yes — but on a private bank loan or line of credit

true.message: Interest paid on government student loans is eligible for a non-refundable credit under ITA s.118.62 at the lowest federal tax rate (15%). Unused interest amounts can be carried forward up to 5 years — use it in the year that produces the greatest tax benefit.

document_checklist_add.document: Student loan interest statement from National Student Loans Service Centre or provincial student loan authority confirming interest paid in 2025

private.message: Interest on private bank loans and lines of credit — even if used to fund education — does not qualify for the student loan interest credit under ITA s.118.62. Only government-issued student loans qualify.

screens.title: Did you or your child withdraw funds from an RESP in 2025?
screens.instruction: Educational Assistance Payments (EAPs) from an RESP — which include accumulated income and government grants — are taxable in the student's hands under ITA s.146.1. The return of contributions is tax-free.

true.message: Educational Assistance Payments (EAPs) from an RESP are taxable in the student's hands under ITA s.146.1(7) — not the subscriber's. The student will receive a T4A showing the EAP amount. Since most students have low income the tax on EAPs is often nil or minimal. The return of original contributions is always tax-free.

document_checklist_add.document: T4A slip for RESP Educational Assistance Payment — issued to the student beneficiary

branch_completion_summary.message: Education section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: T2202 — Tuition and Enrolment Certificate (access via CRA My Account)
Notice of Assessment — confirms tuition carryforward and Canada Training Credit limit
Student loan interest statement from NSLSC or provincial authority
T4A — RESP Educational Assistance Payment slip (issued to student)

intro: Purchasing your first home in 2025 opens several tax benefits — the First Home Buyers' Amount, the First Home Savings Account, and potentially the Home Buyers' Plan. Let's confirm what applies to your situation.

screens.title: Did you purchase a qualifying home in Canada in 2025?
screens.instruction: A qualifying home includes houses, condos, co-operatives, and mobile homes. You must have purchased or built it and it must be your principal place of residence.

options.label: Yes — I completed a home purchase in 2025
No — the purchase has not yet closed

true.message: Home purchase confirmed. Let's confirm eligibility for available credits and plans.

false.message: If the purchase closes in 2026 the credits and plans will apply to your 2026 return. For the FHSA, contributions made in 2025 are still deductible on your 2025 return even if the home is purchased in 2026.

screens.title: Is this your first home — neither you nor your spouse has owned a principal residence in the current year or any of the previous four calendar years?
screens.instruction: Under ITA s.118.05 and s.146.01, first-time buyer status requires that neither you nor your spouse owned a qualifying home as a principal residence at any point in 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, or 2025.

options.label: Yes — neither I nor my spouse owned a principal residence in 2021–2025
No — one of us owned a principal residence during that period

true.message: First-time buyer status confirmed. You may be eligible for the First Home Buyers' Amount credit under ITA s.118.05 and the Home Buyers' Plan RRSP withdrawal under ITA s.146.01.

false.message: First-time buyer status does not apply. The First Home Buyers' Amount and Home Buyers' Plan are not available. The FHSA may still be relevant if you opened one before the purchase.

preparer_flag.flag: Not a first-time buyer — confirm FHSA status and whether HBP applies based on ownership history

unsure.message: Your preparer will confirm eligibility based on your ownership history. Bring details of any property you or your spouse have owned in the past 5 years.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm first-time buyer status — review property ownership history for client and spouse from 2021 to 2025

screens.title: Are you aware of the First Home Buyers' Amount credit?
screens.instruction: The First Home Buyers' Amount under ITA s.118.05 provides a non-refundable federal credit of $10,000 — worth up to $1,500 in federal tax savings. Both spouses may claim it if each qualifies.

options.label: Yes — I plan to claim it

true.message: First Home Buyers' Amount confirmed on your checklist. The $10,000 credit is claimed on Line 31270 of your T1. If you have a spouse you may split the $10,000 claim between your two returns in any way that totals $10,000.

false.message: The First Home Buyers' Amount under ITA s.118.05 gives you a non-refundable $10,000 credit on Line 31270 — worth up to $1,500 in federal tax reduction. It does not need to be repaid and applies to any qualifying home purchase. We have added it to your checklist.

screens.title: Did you withdraw funds from your RRSP under the Home Buyers' Plan (HBP) for this purchase?
screens.instruction: The Home Buyers' Plan under ITA s.146.01 allows first-time buyers to withdraw up to $35,000 from their RRSP tax-free to purchase a qualifying home. Repayments begin two years after the withdrawal year.

options.label: Yes — I withdrew from my RRSP under the HBP
I plan to make an HBP withdrawal for this purchase

true.message: HBP withdrawal confirmed. The withdrawal is tax-free in the year of withdrawal but must be repaid to your RRSP in equal annual instalments beginning in the second calendar year after the year of the first withdrawal. The repayment period is 15 years. Annual repayment amounts are shown on your Notice of Assessment. Missed repayments are added to your income for that year.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RSP slip showing HBP RRSP withdrawal amount — confirm Home Buyers Plan designation on the withdrawal

preparer_flag.flag: HBP withdrawal — confirm repayment start year and annual amount. First repayment due in second year following first withdrawal year.

plan_to.message: To use the HBP the withdrawal must occur before the purchase closes or within 30 days after. You must have a written agreement to buy or build a qualifying home. You will receive a T4RSP showing the withdrawal — do not include it in your RRSP deductions.

preparer_flag.flag: HBP withdrawal planned — confirm eligibility, timing relative to purchase date, and that T4RSP shows correct HBP designation

screens.title: Did you have a First Home Savings Account (FHSA) in 2025?
screens.instruction: The FHSA under ITA s.146.6 allows first-time buyers to contribute up to $8,000 per year (lifetime maximum $40,000) with contributions tax-deductible and qualifying withdrawals tax-free.

options.label: Yes — I contributed to an FHSA in 2025
Yes — I also made a qualifying withdrawal to purchase my home
I opened an FHSA in 2025 but did not contribute yet

contributed.message: FHSA contributions are deductible on your 2025 T1 under ITA s.146.6. The annual limit is $8,000 with a $16,000 first-year limit if you opened the account in 2023 or earlier (unused room carries forward by $8,000 per year). You will receive an FHSA contribution receipt from your financial institution.

document_checklist_add.document: FHSA contribution receipt from financial institution — shows 2025 contributions deductible on T1

withdrew.message: Qualifying FHSA withdrawals to purchase a first home are completely tax-free under ITA s.146.6 — no inclusion in income and no repayment required. You must have been a first-time buyer and the purchase must close by October 1 of the year following the withdrawal. An FHSA T4FHSA slip will be issued showing the withdrawal.

document_checklist_add.document: T4FHSA slip — Statement of First Home Savings Account showing contributions and qualifying withdrawal

opened_only.message: Opening an FHSA without contributing still preserves future contribution room. If the account was opened but no contribution made in 2025, no deduction is available for 2025. You can contribute up to $8,000 in 2026 plus carry-forward room.

false.message: No FHSA noted. If you are a first-time buyer under 71 and do not yet own a home, you may still be eligible to open and contribute to an FHSA — even if you have already purchased. Your preparer can advise.

preparer_flag.flag: Assess FHSA eligibility — client may still qualify to open and deduct contributions if first-time buyer under 71

screens.title: Did you purchase a newly built home or substantially renovated home in 2025?
screens.instruction: Purchasers of newly built or substantially renovated homes may be eligible for the GST/HST New Housing Rebate, which can be worth thousands of dollars.

options.label: Yes — newly built or substantially renovated

true.message: Purchasers of newly built homes priced under $450,000 may claim the GST/HST New Housing Rebate — the federal portion is up to $6,300 and the Ontario provincial portion is up to $24,000 for homes up to $400,000. This rebate is claimed on CRA Form GST190. Bring your purchase agreement and closing documents.

document_checklist_add.document: Purchase agreement and Statement of Adjustments for new construction — required for GST/HST New Housing Rebate application (Form GST190)

preparer_flag.flag: New home construction — assess GST/HST New Housing Rebate eligibility. Federal and Ontario provincial rebates available. Purchase price and occupancy date affect eligibility.

false.message: Resale home noted. GST/HST New Housing Rebate does not apply to resale purchases.

branch_completion_summary.message: First home section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: Purchase agreement and Statement of Adjustments for home purchase
T4RSP slip if HBP RRSP withdrawal was made
FHSA contribution receipt or T4FHSA slip
Form GST190 — New Housing Rebate application if newly built home

intro: Disability-related tax supports require specific CRA certifications and can be claimed retroactively. Let's make sure the Disability Tax Credit, RDSP, and related medical supports are fully addressed.

screens.title: Who has the disability?
screens.instruction: Select all that apply. Different rules apply depending on whether it is you, your spouse, or a dependent.

options.label: My spouse or common-law partner has a disability
A child or dependent of mine has a disability

self.message: We will cover the Disability Tax Credit, RDSP, and attendant care deduction for you.

spouse.message: Your spouse's disability credit may transfer to you if they cannot use the full amount. We will cover the transfer provisions.

child_dependent.message: A dependent's disability credit may transfer to you as the supporting person under ITA s.118.3(2). We will confirm the details.

screens.title: Is there a Disability Tax Credit certificate (Form T2201) approved by CRA for the person with the disability?
screens.instruction: The DTC requires a medical practitioner to certify Form T2201, which CRA must then approve. Without approval the credit cannot be claimed, but approval can be retroactive up to 10 years.

options.label: Yes — CRA has approved Form T2201
Not yet applied — the condition may qualify
The condition does not qualify for the DTC

approved.message: DTC approved. The 2025 federal disability amount is $9,872 — worth up to $1,481 in federal tax. An enhanced amount of $5,758 applies for children under 18. If the eligible person cannot use the full amount against their own tax, the unused portion transfers to a supporting person under ITA s.118.3(2).

document_checklist_add.document: CRA DTC approval letter — confirms certificate period and approved impairment

applied.message: Application pending. If approved before filing, the credit applies from the certificate start date. File your return on time. If approval comes after filing, amend the return — CRA allows retroactive DTC claims up to 10 years.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC application pending — monitor CRA approval. File return on time and amend if approved after filing. Retroactive up to 10 years.

not_applied.message: If the person has a prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts basic activities of daily living, they may qualify for the DTC. Have a medical practitioner complete Form T2201 and submit to CRA. If approved, claims are retroactive up to 10 years — this is a significant opportunity.

preparer_flag.flag: DTC not yet applied for — assess eligibility. If approved, retroactive claim possible up to 10 years. Significant credit opportunity.

ineligible.message: DTC does not apply. We will still check whether attendant care or medical expense claims are available.

screens.title: Did you pay for attendant care, nursing home, or disability support services in 2025?
screens.instruction: Attendant care costs may be deductible under ITA s.64 (as a deduction for earned income) and also claimable as medical expenses under ITA s.118.2(2)(b). You cannot claim the same amount under both provisions.

true.message: Attendant care expenses may be deductible under ITA s.64 as an attendant care deduction — which can exceed the medical expense credit value — or claimed as medical expenses under ITA s.118.2(2)(b). The s.64 deduction requires a DTC certificate and is limited to two-thirds of earned income. Your preparer will determine which claim produces the greater benefit.

document_checklist_add.document: Receipts for attendant care, personal support workers, or nursing home fees paid in 2025 — provider name, address, and amounts paid

preparer_flag.flag: Attendant care — model s.64 deduction vs medical expense claim to determine optimal treatment

screens.title: Does the person with the disability have a Registered Disability Savings Plan (RDSP)?
screens.instruction: An RDSP under ITA s.146.4 is a long-term savings plan that attracts the Canada Disability Savings Grant (up to $3,500/year) and Canada Disability Savings Bond (up to $1,000/year) from the federal government. A DTC approval is required to open an RDSP.

options.label: Yes — an RDSP is open and active
No — but we are interested in opening one

true.message: RDSP contributions attract the Canada Disability Savings Grant (CDSG) of up to $3,500 per year based on family income, and the Canada Disability Savings Bond (CDSB) of up to $1,000 per year for lower-income beneficiaries — both are government contributions requiring no repayment if held for at least 10 years. RDSP contributions are not tax-deductible but grow tax-sheltered.

document_checklist_add.document: RDSP annual statement — shows contributions made and CDSG/CDSB received in 2025

considering.message: An RDSP is one of the most valuable savings tools available for people with disabilities. The federal government contributes grants and bonds worth potentially $90,000 over a lifetime. A DTC approval is the only requirement to open one. Your preparer can provide guidance on the application and optimal contribution strategy.

preparer_flag.flag: RDSP not yet opened — advise on opening process and CDSG/CDSB entitlements. Significant long-term benefit opportunity.

screens.title: Did you pay any medical expenses related to the disability in 2025?
screens.instruction: Disability-specific medical expenses — such as specialized devices, therapies, modifications, and prescriptions — are claimable under ITA s.118.2(2). These are in addition to general medical expenses.

true.message: Disability-related medical expenses are claimable under ITA s.118.2(2) — which covers a broad range of costs including prescribed devices, renovations to accommodate the disability, guide animal care, and specialized therapy. These will be captured in the Deductions Review section. Gather all receipts now.

preparer_flag.flag: Disability-related medical expenses — review ITA s.118.2(2) eligible expense list carefully. Many disability-specific items qualify beyond standard medical costs.

branch_completion_summary.message: Disability section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: CRA DTC approval letter — Form T2201 certification
Attendant care receipts — provider name, address, and amounts paid
RDSP annual statement if an RDSP is open
Medical expense receipts for disability-related costs

intro: Turning 18, 65, or 71 triggers specific tax obligations and planning opportunities. Let's confirm what your milestone means for your 2025 return.

screens.title: Which age milestone did you reach in 2025?

options.label: I am already past 71 — I need to confirm RRIF obligations

turned_18.message: At 18 you become eligible to open a TFSA — you can contribute $7,000 in 2025 plus $7,000 accumulated from 2024. If you worked in prior years your RRSP room (from the prior year's T1) is also available.

turned_65.message: At 65 you become eligible for the Age Amount credit under ITA s.118(2), the Pension Income Credit under ITA s.118(3), pension income splitting under ITA s.60.03, and RRIF conversions. OAS eligibility also begins at 65. We will confirm each item.
turned_65.activates_flag_note: milestone_65 also set for planning purposes

turned_71.message: Turning 71 is the RRSP deadline year. Your RRSP must be converted to a RRIF or annuity by December 31, 2025 under ITA s.146(2)(b.4). You may still contribute to your RRSP in 2025 if you have contribution room — in fact making a final contribution before converting can be beneficial. We will confirm the conversion status.

over_71.message: Post-71 obligations are ongoing — minimum RRIF withdrawals are required each year. We will confirm your minimum withdrawal was taken.

screens.title: Did you open a TFSA in 2025 now that you are 18?

options.label: Yes — I opened and contributed to a TFSA
I opened one but did not contribute yet

true.message: TFSA contributions are not deductible but all growth and withdrawals are completely tax-free. Your 2025 limit is $7,000 plus $7,000 accumulated room from 2024 (if you were 17 at any point in 2024). Over-contribution is subject to a 1% monthly penalty. Confirm your exact contribution room through CRA My Account.

document_checklist_add.document: TFSA contribution receipt from financial institution — confirm 2025 amount contributed

opened.message: Room is accumulating. You can contribute up to $7,000 per year once you are 18 regardless of income. Your total 2025 limit is $7,000 (2025) plus $7,000 (carried over from 2024 if you were a Canadian resident aged 17 in 2024).

false.message: Consider opening a TFSA — it is one of the best savings vehicles available and you can contribute up to $14,000 immediately ($7,000 for 2024 plus $7,000 for 2025).

screens.title: Did you apply for Old Age Security (OAS) when you turned 65?

options.label: Yes — I receive or have applied for OAS
I am deferring OAS to increase my monthly payments

true.message: OAS confirmed. Payments are taxable under ITA s.56(1)(a). If your net income exceeds $90,997 in 2025 the OAS clawback applies under ITA s.180.2. We covered this in the Retirement section.

defer.message: Deferring OAS by up to 5 years (to age 70) increases your monthly payment by 0.6% per month of deferral — a maximum increase of 36% at age 70. This is often advantageous if you have other income and expect to live beyond the breakeven age of approximately 75. Your preparer can model the optimal start age.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS deferral elected — model optimal OAS start age based on current income, expected longevity, and tax bracket

false.message: OAS is not automatic — you must apply. If you are 65 or older and have not applied, contact Service Canada. You may also be entitled to retroactive payments up to 11 months.

preparer_flag.flag: OAS not yet applied for — client should contact Service Canada. Retroactive entitlement up to 11 months.

screens.title: Did you make a final RRSP contribution in 2025 before the December 31 deadline?
screens.instruction: The year you turn 71 is your last year to contribute to your own RRSP. The deadline is December 31 of that year — not the usual 60-day window. A final contribution before conversion is often beneficial.

options.label: Yes — I made a final RRSP contribution before December 31, 2025

true.message: Final RRSP contribution confirmed. This contribution is deductible on your 2025 T1. Bring your RRSP contribution receipt. Note: if you over-contributed you are subject to a 1% monthly penalty on the excess under ITA s.204.1.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP contribution receipt for final 2025 contribution — confirm amount does not exceed available deduction room

preparer_flag.flag: Final RRSP contribution made — confirm contribution room available to avoid over-contribution penalty under ITA s.204.1

false.message: If you had remaining RRSP room and did not contribute in 2025, that room is permanently lost after conversion. Your preparer can confirm whether any room remains for future spousal RRSP contributions if your spouse is under 71.

unsure.message: Check CRA My Account for your 2025 RRSP deduction limit and any contributions recorded. Bring all RRSP receipts to your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: All 2025 RRSP contribution receipts and Notice of Assessment showing available RRSP deduction limit

screens.title: Did you take at least the minimum required RRIF withdrawal in 2025?
screens.instruction: RRIF minimum withdrawals are mandatory each year under ITA s.146.3. The minimum percentage is based on your age on January 1, 2025. Failure to take the minimum results in penalty tax.

options.label: Yes — I took at least the minimum
No — I did not take a withdrawal
I am not sure if the amount was the minimum

true.message: RRIF minimum confirmed. RRIF withdrawals are taxable and reported on a T4RIF slip. Amounts above the minimum may also qualify for the Pension Income Credit under ITA s.118(3) if you are 65 or older.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RIF — Statement of Income from RRIF showing total withdrawal amount in 2025

false.message: Missing the RRIF minimum withdrawal results in a 1% monthly penalty tax under ITA s.146.3 on the shortfall. Contact your financial institution immediately to arrange a catch-up withdrawal and speak with your preparer about penalty mitigation.

preparer_flag.flag: RRIF minimum withdrawal not taken in 2025 — penalty tax may apply under ITA s.146.3. Contact financial institution and advise preparer for mitigation options.

unsure.message: Contact your financial institution to confirm the minimum amount required for 2025 and verify the actual withdrawal. Bring your T4RIF slip to your preparer.

document_checklist_add.document: T4RIF slip and RRIF statement confirming 2025 minimum withdrawal amount taken

branch_completion_summary.message: Age milestones section complete. Here is what we have identified.
highlighted_documents: RRSP contribution receipt for final 2025 contribution if applicable
T4RIF — RRIF withdrawal statement
TFSA contribution receipts if opened at 18

intro: RRSP, TFSA, FHSA, and other registered plans affect nearly every Canadian's T1 return. Let's make sure your contributions, deductions, and any over-contributions are properly addressed.

screens.title: Did you contribute to an RRSP in 2025 or within 60 days of January 1, 2026?
screens.instruction: RRSP contributions are deductible under ITA s.146(1) up to your deduction limit shown on your Notice of Assessment. Contributions made from January 1, 2025 to March 1, 2026 can be claimed on your 2025 return.

true.message: RRSP contributions reduce your taxable income dollar for dollar up to your deduction limit. Your limit is shown on your most recent Notice of Assessment. Contribution receipts are mailed by your financial institution — also available through CRA My Account. Note: you do not have to deduct the full contribution in 2025 — unused contributions can be carried forward to future years when your marginal rate may be higher.

document_checklist_add.document: RRSP contribution receipts for all contributions from March 2, 2025 to March 1, 2026

false.message: No RRSP contributions in 2025. Unused RRSP room accumulates indefinitely — check your most recent Notice of Assessment for your available room.

screens.title: Do you want to deduct the full RRSP contribution on your 2025 return?
screens.instruction: You can contribute to an RRSP now but carry the deduction forward to a future year when your income — and marginal tax rate — may be higher. This is a legitimate and often beneficial planning strategy.

options.label: Yes — deduct the full contribution on my 2025 return
No — I want to carry forward some or all of the deduction
I want my preparer to advise on the optimal timing

true.message: Full deduction on 2025 return confirmed.

false.message: Carrying forward the RRSP deduction is noted. This is beneficial if your income is expected to be higher in a future year — the deduction saves more tax at a higher marginal rate. Your preparer will note the carry-forward amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Client prefers to carry forward some or all RRSP deduction — model optimal deduction year based on income projections

discuss.message: Your preparer will model the deduction timing based on your current and projected future income to maximize tax savings.

preparer_flag.flag: Model RRSP deduction timing — compare current year vs future year tax saving based on marginal rates

screens.title: Did you contribute to a spousal RRSP in 2025?
screens.instruction: A spousal RRSP contribution uses your own deduction room but builds retirement savings in your spouse's name — useful for equalizing retirement income and reducing total tax through pension splitting.

true.message: Spousal RRSP contributions are deductible on your return using your own RRSP room. However if your spouse withdraws from the spousal RRSP within 3 calendar years of any spousal contribution, the withdrawal is attributed back to you as income under ITA s.146(8.3) — the attribution rule. Your preparer must track the last spousal contribution date.

document_checklist_add.document: Spousal RRSP contribution receipt and record of last spousal contribution date — required to assess 3-year attribution rule under ITA s.146(8.3)

preparer_flag.flag: Spousal RRSP contribution — record date of last contribution for 3-year attribution rule under ITA s.146(8.3)

screens.title: Did you contribute to a TFSA in 2025?
screens.instruction: TFSA contributions are not deductible but all growth and withdrawals are tax-free. Over-contributions are subject to a 1% monthly penalty tax on the excess amount under ITA s.207.02.

true.message: TFSA contributions are not tax-deductible and do not appear on your T1. However the 2025 annual contribution limit is $7,000. If you have withdrawn funds from a TFSA in prior years those amounts are added back to your room on January 1 of the following year. Confirm your exact room through CRA My Account to avoid over-contribution penalties.

preparer_flag.flag: Confirm TFSA contribution room through CRA My Account — over-contribution penalty is 1% per month on excess under ITA s.207.02

screens.title: Are you concerned you may have over-contributed to your RRSP?
screens.instruction: RRSP over-contributions above the $2,000 lifetime buffer are subject to a 1% monthly penalty tax under ITA s.204.1. Your deduction limit is on your most recent Notice of Assessment.

options.label: Yes — I may have exceeded my deduction limit

true.message: RRSP over-contributions above a $2,000 lifetime buffer are penalized at 1% per month under ITA s.204.1. If you have over-contributed you must file Form T1-OVP by March 31, 2026 to self-assess the penalty. Remedies include withdrawing the excess (a Form T3012A can reduce withholding tax on the withdrawal) or using deduction room from 2025 earned income if contribution room increases in 2026.

document_checklist_add.document: Form T1-OVP (Individual Tax Return for RRSP, PRPP and SPP Excess Contributions) — required if over-contribution above $2,000 lifetime buffer exists

obligations_add.action: File Form T1-OVP by March 31, 2026 to self-assess RRSP over-contribution penalty under ITA s.204.1 — failure to file adds penalties and interest on top of the 1% monthly excess tax

preparer_flag.flag: RRSP over-contribution concern — verify contribution room immediately via CRA My Account. File T1-OVP by March 31, 2026 if excess exists. Advise on T3012A withdrawal option.

branch_completion_summary.message: Savings and registered plans section complete.
highlighted_documents: RRSP contribution receipts for March 2025 to March 2026 period
Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP deduction limit and TFSA room
Spousal RRSP receipt and last contribution date record

intro: Let's do a final sweep of common deductions that apply to most Canadians — charitable donations, medical expenses, carrying charges, and union dues. These are frequently missed or under-claimed.

screens.title: Did you make charitable donations to registered Canadian charities in 2025?
screens.instruction: Donations to registered Canadian charities are eligible for a non-refundable federal credit under ITA s.118.1 — 15% on the first $200 and 29% on amounts above $200 (33% if your income is above the top federal bracket).

true.message: Charitable donation receipts must be from registered Canadian charities. The federal credit is 15% on the first $200 and 29% on amounts above $200 under ITA s.118.1. Spouses can combine donations on one return to maximize the above-$200 rate. Unused donations can be carried forward up to 5 years. First-time donors (no donation claimed in the prior 5 years) qualify for a 25% First-Time Donor Super Credit on up to $1,000 in cash donations.

document_checklist_add.document: Official charitable donation receipts from registered Canadian charities — showing charity name, registration number, and donation amount

screens.title: Did you pay medical expenses for yourself, your spouse, or your dependents in 2025?
screens.instruction: Medical expenses are claimable under ITA s.118.2 as a non-refundable credit on amounts above 3% of net income (or $2,635, whichever is less). The best 12-month period ending in 2025 or 2026 can be used — it does not have to match the calendar year.

true.message: Medical expenses under ITA s.118.2(2) cover a wide range — prescriptions, dental, vision, physiotherapy, ambulance, home modifications for disability, private health insurance premiums, and many other items. The eligible list is extensive. Only amounts above 3% of your net income (minimum $2,635 in 2025) generate a credit. Pooling all family medical expenses on the lower-income spouse's return often maximizes the claim.

document_checklist_add.document: All medical expense receipts for the best 12-month period ending in 2025 or early 2026 — prescriptions, dental, vision, paramedical services, and private insurance premiums

preparer_flag.flag: Determine best 12-month period for medical expense claim — can span two calendar years. Pool all family expenses on lower-income spouse's return.

screens.title: Did you pay interest on money borrowed to earn investment income, or pay investment management fees in 2025?
screens.instruction: Carrying charges and interest on money borrowed to earn income are deductible under ITA s.20(1)(c) and s.20(1)(bb). This includes interest on investment loans and fees paid for investment advice on taxable accounts.

true.message: Interest on money borrowed to earn investment income is deductible under ITA s.20(1)(c) — for example, a loan used to purchase stocks, bonds, or a rental property. Investment management fees on taxable accounts (not RRSPs, TFSAs, or RRIFs) are deductible under ITA s.20(1)(bb). Fees on registered accounts are not deductible.

document_checklist_add.document: Investment loan interest statements and investment management fee receipts for taxable accounts only

screens.title: Did you pay spousal support under a court order or written agreement in 2025 that has not already been captured?
screens.instruction: This is a catch-all for taxpayers who did not go through the Marital Status branch but still make support payments.

options.label: Yes — I made spousal support payments in 2025

true.message: Spousal support paid periodically pursuant to a court order or written agreement is deductible under ITA s.60(b). Lump sum payments are generally not deductible. Bring your agreement and proof of payments made.

document_checklist_add.document: Separation agreement or court order and records of all spousal support payments made in 2025

screens.title: Do you have your 2024 Notice of Assessment available?
screens.instruction: Your 2024 Notice of Assessment confirms RRSP room, tuition and donation carryforwards, capital loss carryforwards, and other amounts that may affect your 2025 return.

options.label: No — I do not have it

true.message: Your 2024 NOA is a key document. Bring it to your preparer — it confirms your RRSP deduction limit, any carryforward balances, and your account balance with CRA.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — confirms RRSP room, donation carryforward, tuition carryforward, and capital loss carryforward

false.message: Your 2024 Notice of Assessment is available through CRA My Account. If you do not have access your preparer can retrieve it through CRA's Represent a Client portal. It is an important document — try to obtain it before your appointment.

document_checklist_add.document: 2024 Notice of Assessment — retrieve from CRA My Account or ask preparer to access via Represent a Client

highlighted_documents: Official charitable donation receipts from registered charities
All medical expense receipts for the best 12-month period
Investment loan interest statements and investment management fee receipts
2024 Notice of Assessment
branch_completion_summary.transition: Next: Credits Review — the final section.

intro: Let's check the federal and Ontario credits that apply to your situation. Many of these are easy to miss — especially the Ontario-specific credits that are often overlooked.

screens.title: Was your employment or self-employment income under approximately $43,000 in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Canada Workers Benefit under ITA s.122.7 is a refundable credit for low-income workers. The basic amount phases in above $3,000 of earned income and phases out above approximately $23,000 for singles or $36,000 for families.

options.label: Yes — I had modest employment or self-employment income
No — my income was above that range

true.message: You may qualify for the Canada Workers Benefit under ITA s.122.7. The maximum basic amount is $1,590 for single individuals and $2,739 for families in 2025. It is refundable — you receive the credit even if you owe no tax. Advance payments of up to half the annual amount are available through CRA if you apply.

preparer_flag.flag: Assess Canada Workers Benefit eligibility under ITA s.122.7 — confirm earned income and family net income thresholds

false.message: Canada Workers Benefit phase-out threshold exceeded.

screens.title: Did you pay rent or property tax in Ontario in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) under OTA s.103.1 combines three Ontario credits — the Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit, Northern Ontario Energy Credit (if in northern Ontario), and Ontario Sales Tax Credit. Rent and property tax are key inputs.

options.label: I paid property tax directly in 2025
Both — I paid rent for part of the year and property tax for part
Neither — I lived with parents or paid neither

rent.message: Ontario renters may claim the Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit component of the OTB under OTA s.103.1. Eligible rent is the amount paid for a principal residence in Ontario. Subsidized housing rent may be reduced. Bring your landlord's name and address and the total rent paid in 2025.

document_checklist_add.document: Rent receipts or lease showing total rent paid in 2025 and landlord name and address

property_tax.message: Ontario homeowners who paid property tax directly may claim the Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit under OTA s.103.1. Bring your 2025 property tax assessment and payment records.

document_checklist_add.document: 2025 Ontario property tax assessment notice showing annual property tax amount

both.message: For the portion of the year you rented use your rent amount, and for the ownership portion use your property tax amount. The ON-BEN form accommodates split-year claims.

document_checklist_add.document: Rent receipts or lease for rental period and property tax assessment for ownership period

neither.message: You may still qualify for the Ontario Sales Tax Credit component of the OTB based on income alone, even without rent or property tax. Your preparer will include this on the ON-BEN form.

screens.title: Was your individual Ontario employment income under approximately $50,000 in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Low-income Individuals and Families Tax (LIFT) Credit under OTA s.103.4 provides a non-refundable Ontario credit to reduce or eliminate provincial income tax for lower-income workers.

options.label: Yes — my individual income was under approximately $50,000

true.message: The LIFT Credit under OTA s.103.4 reduces Ontario income tax for working individuals with employment income. For 2025 the credit fully eliminates provincial tax up to approximately $30,000 of individual income and phases out as income rises. The credit is based on individual (not family) employment income. Your preparer will calculate the exact amount.

preparer_flag.flag: Assess LIFT Credit under OTA s.103.4 — based on individual employment income, not family income

screens.title: Are you 64 or older and do you own your principal residence in Ontario?
screens.instruction: The Ontario Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant under OTA s.104.1 provides up to $500 per year for Ontario homeowners 64 or older. It is claimed on the ON-BEN form with your T1 return.

options.label: Yes — I am 64 or older and own my Ontario principal residence

true.message: You may qualify for the Ontario Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant under OTA s.104.1 — up to $500 annually. Eligibility requires age 64 or older on December 31, 2025, ownership of your principal residence in Ontario, and net income below $50,000 (for singles) or $60,000 (for couples). It is claimed on the ON-BEN form attached to your T1.

preparer_flag.flag: Assess Ontario Senior Homeowners Property Tax Grant eligibility under OTA s.104.1 — confirm age, ownership, and net income threshold

screens.title: At any point in 2025, did the total cost of all foreign property you owned exceed $100,000 Canadian?
screens.instruction: If the total adjusted cost base of all specified foreign property exceeded $100,000 CAD at any point in 2025, you must file Form T1135 — Foreign Income Verification Statement. Failure to file carries significant penalties.

options.label: Yes — I owned foreign property worth over $100,000 CAD
I am not sure — I have foreign accounts or investments

true.message: Form T1135 — Foreign Income Verification Statement must be filed with your T1 return under ITA s.233.3. Specified foreign property includes foreign bank accounts, foreign stocks, foreign real estate (other than personal-use property), and interests in foreign entities. The penalty for failure to file is $25 per day up to $2,500, plus 5% of the cost of the unreported property for repeated failures. Do not miss this filing.

preparer_flag.flag: T1135 Foreign Income Verification Statement required — specified foreign property exceeded $100,000 CAD. Penalties are severe for failure to file.

unsure.message: If you have foreign bank accounts, investments, or real estate (outside Canada), tally the cost (not current value) of all holdings. If that total exceeded $100,000 Canadian at any point in 2025, T1135 is required. Your preparer will help you determine whether you must file.

preparer_flag.flag: Possible T1135 filing requirement — assess cost of all specified foreign property. Threshold is $100,000 CAD at any point in the year.

screens.title: Did you perform at least 200 hours of volunteer work as a firefighter, search and rescue volunteer, or emergency services volunteer in 2025?
screens.instruction: The Volunteer Firefighters Tax Credit and Search and Rescue Volunteers Tax Credit under ITA s.118.06 and s.118.07 each provide a $3,000 non-refundable credit — worth $450 federally.

options.label: Yes — I completed at least 200 hours of eligible volunteer service

true.message: You may claim the Volunteer Firefighters Tax Credit or Search and Rescue Volunteers Tax Credit under ITA s.118.06 or s.118.07 — a $3,000 amount generating a $450 federal credit. You must have completed a minimum of 200 hours of eligible service in 2025. The certification from your organization is required.

document_checklist_add.document: Certification of volunteer service hours from fire department or search and rescue organization — confirms 200+ eligible hours in 2025

branch_completion_summary.message: Credits review complete. Your interview is now finished. As a reminder: the GST/HST Credit (ITA s.122.5) is a quarterly tax-free payment for low and moderate-income Canadians — it is assessed automatically when you file your T1. No separate application is required.
highlighted_documents: ON-BEN form information — rent or property tax amounts, senior homeowner eligibility
T1135 if specified foreign property exceeded $100,000 CAD
Volunteer service certification if applicable
